findings.title: Plug-in trigram-JS is massively biased upward for finite text and the bias scales with text length N and trigram-vocab size K, so distances are not comparable across languages
findings.what_might_be_wrong: js_div_counts/js_matrix (langtree.py:192-219) use the raw plug-in (MLE) probabilities with NO smoothing or bias correction. Probe 1: between two i.i.d. draws from the IDENTICAL distribution (true JS distance = 0) the measured trigram-JS is 0.62 at alphabet=26/N=5000 and 0.85 at alphabet=80/N=5000 — i.e. the floor of the distance is huge and grows with vocab size. Probe 2: even between two genuinely different distributions, measured JS falls from 0.60 to 0.24 as N grows 5k->200k. In the actual bible_poc headline arm (verse_cap=4000, no char_cap) cleaned text length ranges 2.9x (216k Korean to 620k Indonesian) and distinct trigrams K ranges 53x (1043 to 55496), so the per-language bias is wildly uneven.
findings.why: JS is estimated from finite counts over a sparse high-dimensional trigram space; the plug-in estimator over-separates because unseen/rare trigrams inflate apparent divergence, and the inflation depends on N and K. Languages with large K (CJK, agglutinative) or short text get systematically larger distances to EVERYONE, languages with small K/long text get smaller distances to everyone. K and N correlate with script and morphology, which correlate with family — so the bias is differential along the very axis being tested. Confirmed: in the real headline matrix trigram-JS distances correlate r=0.40 with max(K_i,K_j) and r=0.37 with |K_i-K_j|. A nontrivial fraction of the recovered structure is vocab-size/length artifact, not genealogy.
findings.evidence: langtree.py:192-219 (plug-in, no smoothing); probe1: i.i.d.-same-dist JS=0.62 (a=26,N=5k), 0.85 (a=80,N=5k); probe2: JS 0.60->0.24 as N 5k->200k; real corpus: char-len 216398..619759 (2.9x), K 1043..55496 (53x), N/K 4.1..498; corr(trigramJS, max K)=0.396, corr(trigramJS, |K gap|)=0.370

findings.title: The implemented token bootstrap (bootstrap_ci / branch_support) is invalid: it captures neither the dominant bias nor the true sampling variance, giving falsely tight CIs
findings.what_might_be_wrong: bootstrap_ci (langtree.py:405-433) and branch_support (564-654) resample each language's trigram TOKENS multinomially from its OWN empirical distribution at the SAME total N (tots.append(int(v.sum()))). robustness.py reports these as '95% CI' on purity and RF. Probe 3 on a true-JS=0 pair: the point estimate is 0.567, the bootstrap CI is [0.632,0.642] (centered ABOVE the point, never near the truth 0), while the TRUE sampling distribution from fresh i.i.d. draws is mean 0.569, sd 0.0033; the bootstrap sd is 0.0024 — it UNDER-states the real variance by ~27%.
findings.why: Two compounding errors. (1) Resampling from the plug-in distribution reproduces the same bias in every replicate, so the CI quantifies only Monte-Carlo wobble of an already-biased statistic — it cannot reveal that the estimate is ~0.6 away from truth. (2) The multinomial treats overlapping trigrams as independent draws, violating the dependence structure, which shrinks the apparent variance. Result: the reported error bars are both mis-centered (around the bias) and too narrow, so 'purity 0.77, 95% CI ...' overstates precision and reproducibility. branch_support has the same defect, so bootstrap clade supports are not calibrated either.
findings.evidence: langtree.py:415-417,564-654 (resample at full N from plug-in dist); robustness.py:36-40 reports as 95% CI; probe3: bootstrap CI [0.632,0.642] vs true 95% [0.5625,0.5752]; bootstrap sd 0.0024 vs true sd 0.0033 (ratio 0.73); bootstrap mean 0.637 != true sampling mean 0.569

findings.title: Even the 'corrected' RF denominator cannot reach 0 for a genealogically-perfect tree: a perfect refinement of the gold polytomies scores normRF=0.40
findings.what_might_be_wrong: rf_corrected (langtree.py:436-450) sets denom = (inferred internal bipartitions) + (gold internal bipartitions) and uses dendropy's standard symmetric_difference. But Probe 8 shows a tree that is a PERFECT, non-contradicting binary refinement of the gold (gold's own topology with polytomies arbitrarily resolved) scores rf=31, denom=77, normRF=0.403 vs gold. So the achievable floor for a correct tree is ~0.40, not 0. The README/docstring still frames it as '0 = no conflicting splits'.
findings.why: symmetric_difference counts every inferred split absent from gold as a 'difference', including splits that merely resolve gold's polytomies and contradict nothing. The gold from gold_newick_from_rows lumps deep relationships into big polytomies (e.g. Slavic genus = 9 languages with no internal resolution; 31 IE langs only bucketed by genus), so a binary inferred tree (54 internal splits) is forced to add ~31 'extra' splits that can never match gold's 23. The reported headline normRFs (NJ 0.71, UPGMA 0.79, and the 57-lang 0.87 I reproduced) are therefore on a scale whose best-possible value is ~0.4, not 0 — the absolute numbers still cannot be read as 'fraction of the tree wrong', exactly the prior audit's complaint, but it persists in the new code with a different mechanism.
findings.evidence: langtree.py:436-450; probe7: gold internal bipartitions=23, binary inferred=54, denom=77; probe8: perfect refinement of gold vs gold -> rf=31/77, normRF=0.403; IE genus buckets {Germanic:8,Italic:6,Slavic:9,Baltic:2,Greek:1,Albanian:1,Indo-Iranian:4} mostly unresolved internally

findings.title: Family-NN purity has a high chance floor and is inflated by severe class imbalance (31 of 57 languages are one 'Indo-European' bucket)
findings.what_might_be_wrong: nn_purity (langtree.py:395-402) is reported as the headline (0.65 raw, 0.77 romanized) with no chance baseline in the script. Probe 4 on the real metadata: random-NN family-purity floor = 0.308, and the largest family 'Indo-European' is 31/57 = 54% of all languages. The purity statistic is compared in the README only against the alphabet baseline (0.44), never against this 0.31 chance floor or a permutation null.
findings.why: When one class holds 54% of the sample, any distance that merely separates the big block from everything else scores high purity almost for free: a random IE language's nearest neighbour is overwhelmingly likely to be another IE language by abundance alone. The 'Indo-European' bucket also lumps Germanic+Romance+Slavic+Indo-Iranian+Greek+Albanian, so a Hindi->English or Russian->Greek nearest-neighbour counts as a family 'hit' despite being a 5000-year-deep split. So the 0.65/0.77 numbers conflate 'recovers genealogy' with 'separates the dominant block and exploits class imbalance', and the margin over the 0.31 floor is much smaller than the margin over the 0.44 alphabet baseline implies.
findings.evidence: langtree.py:395-402 (purity); bible_poc.py:146-153 reports purity with no chance line; probe4: floor=0.308, family sizes {Indo-European:31, Austronesian:5, Afro-Asiatic:4, Niger-Congo:4, Dravidian:3, Uralic:2, Sino-Tibetan:2, plus 6 singletons}; reproduced headline purity 0.649 (trigram) vs 0.439 (alphabet)

findings.title: ASJP agreement r=0.78 is reported with an invalid Pearson p over non-independent pairs and no test isolating fine (within-block) structure from shared script/major-family blocks
findings.what_might_be_wrong: asjp_tree.py:116-118 reports the distance-matrix correlation r between the text tree and ASJP via np.corrcoef over the C(k,2) flattened upper-triangle, with no permutation/Mantel test and no within-family control. Probe 6: two distance matrices that share ONLY coarse 5-block (script/major-family) structure with independent fine noise already correlate r=0.71; the naive Pearson p over 435 pairwise entries returns 1.1e-67 despite the pairs being non-independent (each language appears in k-1 pairs).
findings.why: Pearson significance assumes independent observations, but pairwise distances share languages, so the effective degrees of freedom are far below C(k,2) and the naive p-value is meaningless (a Mantel permutation test is required). More importantly, a high r can be produced entirely by both matrices agreeing on coarse blocks (which script / which major family) without any agreement on fine genealogy — exactly what trigram-JS and ASJP both trivially encode. So r=0.78 does not establish that the information-theoretic text method recovers the SAME fine structure as the wordlist standard; it is consistent with both methods merely separating the obvious blocks.
findings.evidence: asjp_tree.py:116-118 (corrcoef, no Mantel, naive interpretation); probe6: block-only-shared matrices give r=0.709; naive Pearson p=1.1e-67 over 435 non-independent pairs

findings.title: n-gram order is effectively unjustified by estimation theory: higher n increases sparsity/bias yet the sweep is read as 'stable', masking that low orders carry the signal
findings.what_might_be_wrong: robustness.py:23-33 runs the n=1..5 sweep and concludes 'family-NN is stable across n ... higher orders don't magically help, low orders already carry the signal'. But the headline method is n=3, and the same script reports unigram-JS normRF identical to trigram-JS (I reproduced normRF=0.870 for BOTH n=1 and n=3 at the family/UPGMA level, with purity 0.632 vs 0.649). At n=3 the trigram estimate is sparse (real N/K as low as 4.1 tokens per distinct trigram), so the plug-in bias is near-maximal exactly where the method claims to operate.
findings.why: If unigram-JS (frequencies only, far less bias) matches trigram-JS on the tree metric, the higher-order information-theoretic content is not what is driving the result — the stated contribution ('order/n-gram structure recovers genealogy') is not demonstrated, and the order choice n=3 is not motivated by any estimator-quality criterion (held-out perplexity / entropy wall), only asserted. The prior audit flagged n=3 as the RF-argmin; the new point is that the replacement justification ('stable across n') actually shows the trigram machinery adds little over unigrams while incurring the worst bias.
findings.evidence: robustness.py:23-33 ('stable across n'); reproduced: trigram normRF 0.870 / purity 0.649 vs unigram normRF 0.870 / purity 0.632 (57-lang arm); real N/K as low as 4.1 (extreme trigram sparsity)

findings.title: A topologically CORRECT tree cannot score 0: the achievable normRF floor on the real 57-taxa gold is 0.403, but headline RF is reported as if 0 = perfect
findings.what_might_be_wrong: rf_corrected's docstring claims 'a binary tree merely refining gold's polytomies is not over-penalised' and 'normalized in [0,1]; 0 = no conflicting splits'. This is false. The gold tree from gold_newick_from_rows is heavily polytomous (only 23 non-trivial bipartitions for 57 taxa), while any UPGMA/NJ tree is binary (54 bipartitions). I resolve_polytomies'd the REAL gold into a perfectly-CONSISTENT binary tree (zero conflicting splits) and rf_corrected returned normRF=0.403, not 0. So the best score ANY method can achieve is ~0.40; the headline NJ 0.71 / UPGMA 0.79 are being implicitly read against an unreachable 0.
findings.why: RF symmetric difference counts every bipartition present in one tree but not the other. A binary refinement adds ~31 bipartitions that simply don't exist in the polytomous gold, and the corrected denominator (inferred 54 + gold 23 = 77) still divides by them, so rf>=31 always -> norm>=0.40. Reporting 0.71 without stating the 0.40 floor makes the tree look ~3x further from truth than it can possibly be, and the docstring's '0 = no conflicting splits' actively misleads. On the achievable [0.40, 1.00] scale, 0.71 maps to ~0.51 -- about halfway between perfect-given-gold and random, NOT 'close to truth'.
findings.evidence: /private/tmp probe: resolve_polytomies on real gold -> rf_corrected normRF=0.403 (rf=31, denom=77); toy: polytomy gold '((a,b,c),(d,e,f))' vs CORRECT refinement '(((a,b),c),((d,e),f))' -> norm=0.500; star gold '(a,b,c,d,e)' vs binary -> norm=1.000. langtree.py:436-450 (rf_corrected docstring '0 = no conflicting splits') and langtree.py:253-276 (gold is genus/subgenus polytomies). gold has 23 non-trivial bipartitions vs binary 54.

findings.title: README/notebook claim the random-tree null is '~0.97' but on the actual 57-taxa polytomous gold the null is saturated at p50=1.000
findings.what_might_be_wrong: README.md ('a random-tree null sits at ~0.97' line 27; 'Read RF against the ~0.97 random null' line 62), robustness.py:40 ('random-tree null normRF ~0.97'), and build_notebook.py:302 ('random ~= 0.97') all hard-code 0.97 as the chance level. Running the project's own random_tree_null on the real gold gives p05=p50=p95=1.000 (min~0.974). The 0.97 is stale (likely from the old 14-taxon binary-ish Tier-1 gold or the 2(n-3) denominator) and contradicts the function the live notebook cell actually calls.
findings.why: Because gold is polytomous and rf_corrected's denominator includes the random tree's own ~54 bipartitions, a random binary tree shares essentially no splits with gold, so rf ~= 54+23 = 77 = denom -> norm ~= 1.0. Quoting 0.97 understates the ceiling and, combined with the unstated 0.40 floor, distorts the 'far above chance' narrative: the honest window is [0.40, 1.00], and 0.71 is only ~half-way down it, not 'far below a 0.97 chance line'. A reader who anchors on 0.97 will think 0.71 is a small improvement over chance when the correct anchor is 1.00 (and the correct floor is 0.40).
findings.evidence: /private/tmp: lt.random_tree_null(names, gold, n=300/500) on real 57-taxa gold -> (p05,p50,p95,min) = (1.000,1.000,1.000,0.974). Contradicts README.md:27,62; robustness.py:40; build_notebook.py:302. Note build_notebook.py:243-248 DOES compute it live, so the rendered notebook will print 1.000 while the surrounding prose says 0.97.

findings.title: Family-level nn_purity is measured at the coarsest rank where Indo-European is 54% of taxa; random-NN already scores 0.308 and the metric gives no credit for sub-family structure
findings.what_might_be_wrong: nn_purity (langtree.py:395-402) counts a hit whenever a language's nearest neighbour shares its top-level Family. With 31/57 languages Indo-European, a Hindi-Russian or Romanian-Swedish nearest-neighbour both count as 'family hits' despite being deep IE splits. The headline 'trigram-JS family purity 0.65' is dominated by the easy IE-vs-everything-else cut, and the gold's genus/subgenus resolution (Germanic/Romance/Slavic -- the structure the method should be tested on) is invisible to this metric.
findings.why: Random-NN family purity on this exact distribution is 0.308 (sum_l (size(fam_l)-1)/(N-1) / N), driven by IE's within-family hit prob 30/56=0.54. So the real lift of 0.65 over chance is only ~0.34, and almost all of it is achievable by any method that separates scripts/IE-from-non-IE -- exactly what the dumb alphabet baseline (0.44) already partly does. Quoting 0.65 vs 0.44 without the 0.31 chance line overstates how much genuine genealogical structure is recovered, and using top-level Family masks whether Germanic and Romance are actually separated.
findings.evidence: /private/tmp: chance nn_purity baseline = 0.308; IE = 31/57 = 0.54 of taxa. langtree.py:395-402 keys on group[label] which bible_poc.py:141 / flores_replicate.py:172 set to Family (top level). Headline 0.65 vs alphabet 0.44 vs chance 0.31.

findings.title: Six singleton-family languages cap nn_purity at 0.895, and Turkish vs Korean are split across 'Altaic' / 'Altaic(?)' so any Turkic-Altaic link is scored a miss
findings.what_might_be_wrong: In the 57-language gold, 6 languages (Turkish, Korean, Japanese, Vietnamese, Thai, Basque) are the sole members of their Family, so they can NEVER have a same-family nearest neighbour -- nn_purity is structurally capped at 51/57 = 0.895, not 1.0. Worse, the metadata gives Turkish Family='Altaic' but Korean Family='Altaic(?)' (a different string with a question mark), so even a perfect Turkish-Korean grouping is counted as a family MISMATCH.
findings.why: Singleton families guarantee misses regardless of method quality, deflating the absolute purity number and making it non-comparable across language panels (the ceiling depends on how many singletons are in the set). The Altaic vs 'Altaic(?)' string mismatch means the project's own gold penalizes the very 'Turkic/Altaic' recovery it might claim, and silently treats a debated macro-family as two families -- an inconsistent gold standard that biases RF and purity against any deep East-Asian grouping. These are baked into both the headline purity AND the gold RF, so they distort both metrics in a way better distance estimation cannot fix.
findings.evidence: /private/tmp: 6 singleton families -> purity ceiling 0.895; metadata.csv: Turkish Family='Altaic', Korean Family='Altaic(?)', Japanese Family='Japonic'. gold_newick_from_rows (langtree.py:253) and fam_of in bible_poc.py:141 use these raw strings.

findings.title: UPGMA's ultrametric (molecular-clock) tree is the one fed to cophenetic_corr, bootstrap_ci AND branch_support; NJ is the headline topology but the reliability machinery never uses it
findings.what_might_be_wrong: Although nj_newick exists and the README headline is 'NJ beats UPGMA (0.71 vs 0.79)', every downstream reliability/diagnostic function builds its trees with upgma(): bootstrap_ci (langtree.py:430), branch_support (langtree.py:590,637), cophenetic_corr (langtree.py:368), and the plotted dendrograms (bible_poc.py:177, flores_replicate.py:306) are all UPGMA. So the bootstrap CIs, branch supports, and the cophenetic 0.91 all describe the WORSE, clock-assuming tree, not the headline NJ tree.
findings.why: UPGMA assumes a constant rate of orthographic change across all lineages (an ultrametric). Character-stat change is manifestly non-clocklike (deep-orthography English vs shallow Finnish; abjad vs alphabet), so UPGMA systematically mis-roots and mis-joins unequal-rate lineages. Reporting NJ as the headline topology while deriving all stability/quality numbers from UPGMA means the confidence intervals and branch supports do not correspond to the advertised tree; a reader cannot tell whether the NJ clades are well supported. The cophenetic 0.91 specifically measures UPGMA's fit to its own distances, irrelevant to NJ's reliability.
findings.evidence: langtree.py: bootstrap_ci uses upgma (l.430), branch_support uses upgma (l.590,637), cophenetic_corr uses upgma (l.368). nj_newick (l.322) is only called for the headline RF number in build_notebook.py:241/245; no bootstrap/support is computed for the NJ topology.

findings.title: NJ runs on Jensen-Shannon distances, which are non-additive, and silently clamps the resulting negative branch lengths -- the NJ topology has no consistency guarantee
findings.what_might_be_wrong: nj_newick (langtree.py:322-360) implements Saitou-Nei NJ, whose optimality/consistency guarantee requires additive (tree-realizable, 4-point-condition) distances. JS distances are a metric but are NOT additive. The code writes branch lengths with max(.,0) clamps (langtree.py:351,360), so negative lengths arising from non-additivity are hidden rather than flagged.
findings.why: On non-additive input, NJ's neighbour-selection (Q-criterion) can pick wrong pairs, so the recovered topology has no consistency guarantee and is sensitive to small distance perturbations -- yet the README presents NJ's 0.71 as a principled improvement over UPGMA. The negative-branch clamp masks the warning sign of an additivity violation (I produced clamped near-zero branches on a deliberately non-additive 4x4). This weakens the 'NJ is the proper, assumption-free method' framing: NJ merely trades the clock assumption for an additivity assumption that JS also violates.
findings.evidence: /private/tmp: NJ on a non-additive 4x4 returned all-positive branches only because max(la,0)/max(dget,0) zeroed a negative la (langtree.py:351,360). JS is the input (build_notebook.py:239 D=Djs_rom -> nj_newick). No additivity check or negative-length warning anywhere.

findings.title: RF is never placed on its true achievable scale: the floor (0.40) is not computed and the null (1.00) is mis-quoted, so all method/corpus comparisons use the wrong yardstick
findings.what_might_be_wrong: The pieces exist (rf_corrected, random_tree_null) but no code computes/reports the achievable FLOOR (best-possible RF given gold's polytomies), and the null is mis-quoted as 0.97 while actually 1.00. The project reports 0.71/0.79 against an implicit [0,1] scale whose true endpoints for THIS gold are [0.403, 1.000]. The prior audit flagged the denominator (now partly fixed via sum-of-bipartitions) but did not establish that the polytomous gold imposes a hard 0.40 floor the corrected denominator does NOT remove.
findings.why: Without the floor, every comparison (JS vs NCD, NJ vs UPGMA, Bible vs FLORES) is read against the wrong endpoints. The 0.08 gap between NJ 0.71 and UPGMA 0.79 looks like 'NJ ~10% better', but on the achievable [0.40,1.00] window it is (0.71-0.40)/0.60=0.52 vs (0.79-0.40)/0.60=0.65 -- a larger relative gap; and the absolute numbers look like failures (0.71 'wrong') when they are ~half-way to the best attainable. Mis-scaling propagates into every headline conclusion about which method/corpus wins. The cheap fix is to report (observed, floor-from-resolved-gold, null-p50) as a triple, or rescale to (observed-floor)/(null-floor).
findings.evidence: /private/tmp: floor=0.403 (resolved gold), null p50=1.000; rescaled 0.71 -> 0.51. No langtree.py function computes the floor; random_tree_null returns percentiles but the prose (README.md:27,62) anchors to a stale 0.97; build_notebook.py:248 prints p05/p50 but never the floor.

findings.title: Family-purity headline is dominated by the 31/57 Indo-European bloc; on the genealogically hard non-IE half it barely beats chance
findings.what_might_be_wrong: The headline 'trigram-JS family purity 0.65 (live: 0.684)' is an average over a panel that is 54% Indo-European (31/57), and IE languages are nearly all same-script (Latin/Cyrillic/Greek). Splitting the metric: IE-only purity = 29/31 = 0.935, but non-IE-only purity = 10/26 = 0.385 (raw) — only marginally above the random-NN chance floor of 0.308. The single number conflates 'recovers IE' (easy, script-aided) with 'recovers families in general' (the actual claim), and the easy majority class carries the headline.
findings.why: NN family-purity counts a hit whenever a language's nearest neighbour is any same-family language. With 31 IE languages packed into one Latin/Cyrillic/Greek script space, almost every IE language trivially finds another IE language nearby, so 29/31 hits are nearly guaranteed by script+areal proximity, not by genealogical signal. Because IE is 54% of the panel, those near-automatic hits inflate the pooled average to 0.68 even though the method fails on most non-IE families. The 'beats the alphabet baseline 0.44' framing inherits the same inflation.
findings.evidence: Live on 57 langs (verse_cap=2000, char_cap=30000): pooled trigram purity 0.684; IE-only 29/31=0.935; non-IE-only 10/26=0.385; chance floor sum c_f(c_f-1)/n(n-1)=0.308 (bible_poc.py:146-153, langtree.py:395-402).

findings.title: RAW cross-script nearest-neighbours are pure selection-order tie-breaks at JS=1.000 (the 'Amharic magnet'), not findings
findings.what_might_be_wrong: For every single-script language (Kannada, Telugu, Malayalam, Thai, Burmese, Korean, Greek), the trigram-JS distance is 1.000000 (within 1e-6) to ALL 56 other languages — a total tie. nn_purity (langtree.py:400) and bible_poc's argsort (bible_poc.py:149) resolve the tie by returning the first index in SELECTION order, which happens to land on 'Amharic'. The raw-arm dendrogram's entire non-Latin/Cyrillic structure, every raw cross-script NN, and the raw non-IE purity number are therefore artifacts of dict/list ordering, carrying zero linguistic information.
findings.why: Languages in disjoint scripts share no character trigrams, so JS divergence saturates at its maximum (distance 1.0) for all cross-script pairs. When a language is at the identical max distance from everything, the 'nearest neighbour' is undefined; min/argmin silently picks the first-encountered element. So 'Greek's NN is Amharic' or any raw cross-script branching is an arbitrary by-product of the order languages were listed in, masquerading as a measured relationship. The whole 'raw clusters by script' / raw-arm topology narrative rests on a degeneracy.
findings.evidence: Live: median pairwise JS=1.000; 923/1596 pairs >=0.999; Kannada/Telugu/Thai/Korean/Greek/Burmese each min-dist=1.000000 with 56 langs tied within 1e-6; NN='Amharic' for all, unchanged when SELECTION order is reversed (langtree.py:200-208 js_div_counts returns 1.0 on disjoint vocab; line 400 min-key tie-break).

findings.title: Family-purity is never reported against its ~0.31 chance floor; the README implies a floor of 0 and misapplies the 0.97 random-tree null to it
findings.what_might_be_wrong: README quotes purity 0.65/0.77/0.63 vs alphabet 0.44/0.47/0.34 with no purity-specific null. The only null mentioned (~0.97) is the random-TREE-TOPOLOGY null for normalized RF, which says nothing about the NN-purity metric. The honest random-NN purity floor for this 57-lang panel is 0.308 (set by family-size imbalance), so the dumb alphabet baseline (0.44) is only ~0.13 above chance and the romanized arm's '+0.30 margin' is measured over the baseline, not over chance. A reader naturally reads 0.65 vs an implied 0 floor.
findings.why: NN purity has a non-zero expectation under random assignment equal to the probability two random taxa share a family, which for an imbalanced panel (54% IE) is 0.31, not 0. Reporting the raw fraction without this floor overstates how much structure is recovered, and pairing it only with the RF random-tree null (a different metric) lets the strong-looking 0.97 number rub off on the purity claim. The 'information theory earns its keep' argument is weakened once both bars are measured from 0.31 rather than 0.
findings.evidence: README.md:19-32 (purity numbers + '+0.30 margin'), README.md:27 ('random-tree null sits at ~0.97' is the RF null in langtree.py:383-392 random_tree_null, which only scores RF vs gold, never purity); computed chance floor 0.308; grep of README found no purity floor (~0.31) anywhere.

findings.title: Gold tree treats discredited/uncertain groupings as hard families: Turkish='Altaic' and Korean='Altaic(?)' are separate singletons, so the Altaic NN that occurs is scored as a miss
findings.what_might_be_wrong: gold_newick_from_rows builds families from the corpus metadata's raw Family strings. 'Altaic' (Turkish) and 'Altaic(?)' (Korean) are distinct strings, so the gold tree places Turkish and Korean in two different singleton families, and the family-purity metric counts a Turkish<->Korean nearest-neighbour as a MISS — even though Turkic-Koreanic grouping is exactly the (now-rejected) Altaic hypothesis. 'Altaic' itself is a discredited macro-family; using it as a gold label both inflates the singleton count (6 singletons -> max achievable purity capped at 0.895) and makes the gold standard internally inconsistent (Turkish labelled Altaic, Korean labelled Altaic-with-a-question-mark, Japanese labelled Japonic).
findings.why: The gold tree is the ground truth that RF and family-purity score against. If the labels encode a non-standard, internally inconsistent taxonomy (Altaic as a family for one language but not its supposed relative), then both 'hits' and the achievable ceiling are wrong: genuinely related placements can be scored as errors and the metric can never reach 1.0 because 6 families have exactly one member and can never get a same-family NN. The validation target is contestable in precisely the cells where the method is weakest (the non-IE singletons).
findings.evidence: Metadata Family strings: Turkish='Altaic', Korean='Altaic(?)', Japanese='Japonic' (distinct singletons). gold_newick_from_rows keys on fam string verbatim (langtree.py:253-276). 6 singleton families -> max purity 51/57=0.895; Turkish's actual raw NN is Indonesian and Korean's is Amharic (both ties), but any Turkish-Korean adjacency would still score as a cross-family miss.

findings.title: 102-language breadth claim uses align=False, comparing full Bibles (OT content) against NT-only Bibles (NT content) — content is not controlled and is confounded with family
findings.what_might_be_wrong: scale_up.py runs bc.load(align=False), where each language takes sorted(verse_ids)[:4000]. Verse IDs sort lexicographically: full Bibles start at 'b.1CH...' (1 Chronicles, OT) while NT-only Bibles start at 'b.1CO...' (1 Corinthians, NT). So full-Bible languages are profiled on different books than NT-only languages. Worse, which languages have full vs NT-only Bibles correlates with family: major European (IE) languages tend to have full Bibles, indigenous/minority (non-IE) languages are disproportionately NT-only. The breadth headline '102 langs, purity 0.63 vs baseline 0.34' therefore mixes a content split with the family split it claims to recover.
findings.why: If two languages are compared on different scripture content (OT prose+genealogies vs NT epistles), part of their character-trigram distance is topical/lexical difference of content, not language difference — the exact confound the verse-aligned design was meant to remove. When the content split (full vs NT) is correlated with the family split (IE vs non-IE), the method can appear to separate families partly because it is separating OT-content languages from NT-content languages. The breadth result is thus not a clean replication of the controlled 57-lang finding.
findings.evidence: scale_up.py:29 load(align=False); biblecorpus.py:88-91 vids=sorted(verses[fn])[:verse_cap]. Live: English/Greek (full) first sorted id 'b.1CH.1.1'; Swahili-NT/Latvian-NT first sorted id 'b.1CO.1.1' (different book). Lexicographic sort also breaks canonical order (1.10 before 1.2).

findings.title: 'Semitic recovered' (Hebrew-Arabic-Syriac) is substantially a uroman vowelless-skeleton artifact: romanized abjads keep ~10-20% vowels vs ~46-50% for Romance
findings.what_might_be_wrong: After romanization, Hebrew, Arabic and Syriac become mutual nearest neighbours and the README frames this as Semitic family recovery (downgraded but still claimed: 'the Semitic pair stays genuinely closer than chance'). But uroman renders the Hebrew and Arabic abjads as near-vowelless consonant skeletons (live: romanized vowel fraction Hebrew 0.100, Arabic 0.205) while giving full-vowel output to every other language (Spanish 0.462, Greek 0.502). A shared, conspicuous, non-genealogical property — 'almost no vowels' — is imposed on exactly the pair being claimed as recovered, and a low-vowel consonant string is automatically trigram-close to another low-vowel consonant string regardless of genealogy.
findings.why: Character-trigram JS is dominated by the most frequent characters; vowels are the highest-frequency characters in most languages. If two texts both drop ~80-90% of their vowels, their trigram inventories collapse onto a similar consonant-cluster space, manufacturing similarity from the romanization scheme rather than from shared inheritance. The controls.py devowel test is the right idea, but the headline/README still presents the Semitic reconnection as partly genuine; the asymmetry of the transform across the compared pair means even the residual closeness can't be cleanly attributed to genealogy.
findings.evidence: Live romanized: Hebrew-Arabic JS 0.763, Hebrew NN=Arabic, Arabic NN=Syriac, Syriac NN=Arabic; vowel fractions Hebrew 0.100 / Arabic 0.205 vs Spanish 0.462 / Italian 0.470 / Greek 0.502 (uroman cache v2000_c30000; controls.py:40-50 devowel test confirms the mechanism).

findings.title: ASJP cross-check is less independent than framed: it compares the ROMANIZED text tree to a phonetic transcription, so both arms have removed the script confound by transliteration
findings.what_might_be_wrong: README: 'two completely different methods recover the same structure, r=0.78.' But asjp_tree.py correlates ASJP (a Latin-letter phonetic ASJPcode transcription) against the uroman-ROMANIZED trigram-JS matrix, not the raw text. Both inputs are transliterations into a shared Latin-ish symbol space, so part of the agreement is that two romanization/transcription pipelines of the same languages agree, not that an orthographic method and a phonetic method independently converge. The within-IE correlation is genuinely high (live r=0.855), so there is real signal — but 'completely different methods' overstates the independence.
findings.why: Independence is the whole point of a cross-check. If the text arm used for the correlation is the romanized arm, then both the text distances and the ASJP distances are functions of a sound/letter-collapsed representation of the same wordforms; shared translation/transcription conventions and the shared collapse of scripts inflate the correlation beyond what an orthography-vs-phonology comparison would show. The claim should be scoped to 'romanized text vs ASJP agree, strongly within IE.'
findings.evidence: asjp_tree.py:99-103 Dtext = js_matrix on rom_keep (romanized), correlated with Dasjp; live r(ASJP,romanized-text)=0.776, IE-only r=0.855; the RAW-text arm (the genuinely orthographic one) is never correlated against ASJP.

findings.title: NN family-purity headline (0.65) is benchmarked against the wrong baseline: random-NN chance is 0.31, and one giant 31-language Indo-European bucket supplies most of the score
findings.what_might_be_wrong: The crux metric, nearest-neighbour FAMILY purity, uses the Bible metadata 'Family' column, where 31 of 57 languages (54%) are lumped into a single 'Indo-European' family. Any IE language whose NN is any other IE language (e.g. German->Hindi, Russian->Greek, Latin->Marathi) counts as a 'hit' across genealogically very deep splits. I measured: actual trigram-JS purity = 0.667; random-NN chance baseline = 0.308 (not ~0); IE languages alone are right 29/31 times, contributing 29/57 = 0.51 of the 0..1 score by themselves. The honest within-IE test (genus-level labels: Germanic/Romance/Slavic/...) drops purity to 0.596 against a chance of only 0.067.
findings.why: Comparing 0.65 only to the alphabet baseline (0.44) and to 0 hides that ~0.31 of the score is free from a single oversized class. Family-level purity rewards 'is this an IE language next to another IE language', which a script/inventory signal alone achieves, so the metric overstates how much genealogy (let alone information theory) is recovered. The defensible number is the genus-level 0.60, which is LOWER than the advertised 0.65 and should be the headline.
findings.evidence: biblecorpus.py SELECTION + metadata.csv -> family sizes {Indo-European:31, Austronesian:5, Afro-Asiatic:4, Niger-Congo:4, Dravidian:3, Uralic:2, Sino-Tibetan:2, +6 singletons}; diagnostic: chance=0.308, trigram-JS family purity=0.667, genus purity=0.596 (chance 0.067), IE-NN-is-IE = 29/31.

findings.title: Gold-standard taxonomy is internally inconsistent and uses discredited families: 'Altaic' vs 'Altaic(?)' splits Turkish from Korean by a literal typo; 6 singleton families cap purity
findings.what_might_be_wrong: The gold tree and family-purity labels come straight from the christos-c metadata.csv Family column, unvetted. Turkish is Family='Altaic' and Korean is Family='Altaic(?)' -- two DIFFERENT strings, so the pipeline scores them as different families even though the metadata intends the (now-rejected) Altaic grouping that would unite them. Korean is also Family='Altaic(?)' and Japanese 'Japonic' (both contested). Six languages (Turkish, Korean, Japanese, Vietnamese, Thai, Basque) are singleton families that can NEVER score a same-family NN, structurally capping purity at 0.895 regardless of method quality. Icelandic's metadata Script is literally 'Ethiopic' (a data error; Icelandic is Latin).
findings.why: Using an un-audited, partly-discredited (Altaic) and typo-ridden taxonomy as ground truth means both the RF score and family-purity are measured against an unreliable target. The Altaic/Altaic(?) string mismatch silently penalizes the one Turkic-Koreanic relationship the metadata meant to assert; the singleton ceiling means part of the gap from 1.0 is unattainable by construction, not method error; the Icelandic Script error would mis-route any script-dependent logic (uroman lcode/script handling).
findings.evidence: metadata.csv: Turkish=Altaic, Korean='Altaic(?)', Japanese=Japonic, Icelandic Script=Ethiopic; diagnostic confirmed fam_of['Turkish']!=fam_of['Korean'] and 6 singleton families -> purity ceiling 1-6/57=0.895.

findings.title: Cross-corpus FLORES replication runs Serbian in DIFFERENT scripts for the two corpora, breaking the 'same language, identical pipeline' control
findings.what_might_be_wrong: flores_replicate.py claims it runs the 'identical pipeline ... on the SAME language set' so any agreement proves the tree is a property of languages not register. But the Serbian Bible text is in LATIN script (verified: 'Selek Amonac, Naraj Viroćanin...') while FLORES only ships srp_Cyrl (verified: 'У понедељак су научници...'). pick_flores_stem falls back to Cyrillic. So in the raw arm Serbian is compared as Latin in one corpus and Cyrillic in the other -- its trigram vocabulary is disjoint between corpora and its tree placement cannot 'replicate'. Icelandic is rescued only because its metadata script ('Ethiopic') is wrong and the fallback picks Latn by luck.
findings.why: The whole point of the FLORES arm is an apples-to-apples register control on the SAME representation. A within-control script switch for Serbian means at least one language's cross-corpus 'agreement' or 'disagreement' is a script artifact, not a register result, weakening the 'family tree survives outside the biblical register' claim and any per-language stability statement.
findings.evidence: flores_replicate.py SCRIPT_TO_15924 + pick_flores_stem (lines 80-92); metadata Serbian Script=Latin; ls corpus/flores200_dataset/dev shows only srp_Cyrl.dev (no srp_Latn); verified Bible-vs-FLORES sample text scripts differ.

findings.title: FLORES (~2009 sentences) is far too small for character-trigram distributions over large-inventory scripts: Chinese K/N=0.77, Korean K/N=0.37 -- near-pure-noise estimates
findings.what_might_be_wrong: The FLORES raw arm estimates trigram distributions from dev+devtest (~2009 sentences). For alphabetic scripts this is fine (English K/N=0.021), but for large-inventory scripts the trigram space is hopelessly under-sampled: Chinese has 62,938 distinct trigrams from only 81,936 tokens (K/N=0.768 -- almost every trigram occurs once), Korean K/N=0.365. The JS distance involving these languages is computed over distributions that are essentially singletons, so the estimate is dominated by sampling noise.
findings.why: Jensen-Shannon distance between two near-singleton empirical distributions is driven by which rare trigrams happened to appear, not by the underlying language statistics. CJK/Korean/Japanese placements in the FLORES tree are therefore artifacts of corpus size, and the FLORES 'replication' inherits unstable distances exactly where the corpus is smallest. The prompt's data-sufficiency worry is confirmed quantitatively and is distinct from the prior audit (which only covered UDHR byte-truncation).
findings.evidence: Diagnostic on FLORES dev+devtest via langtree.ngram_counter(n=3): zho_Hans N=81936 K=62938 K/N=0.768; kor_Hang K/N=0.365; vs eng_Latn 0.021, deu 0.023, rus 0.032.

findings.title: Romanized arm budget is set BEFORE uroman expansion, so the 'inventory held constant' arm compares languages on 2.2x-unequal data volumes
findings.what_might_be_wrong: In flores_replicate.py the char_cap=30000 (and the Bible romanized arm) is applied to the RAW pre-romanization text; uroman is then run and clean() applied to the output. But romanization EXPANDS character count unevenly: in the cached flores_bible_v2000_c30000 arm, Latin-script languages stay at 30,000 chars while Korean expands to 65,542, Japanese 56,480, Amharic 56,309, Chinese 50,562 -- a 2.19x max/min imbalance. The romanized arm is the one that produces the headline 0.77 purity.
findings.why: Languages whose romanizations are 2x longer have far better-estimated, lower-variance trigram distributions than those held at 30k. Unequal token budgets bias JS distances (the plug-in/JS estimator is sensitive to N), and the romanized arm's headline rise to 0.77 is partly a finite-sample effect, not purely the 'one common alphabet' effect the framing credits. The 'inventory held constant' control holds the alphabet constant but lets the DATA BUDGET vary, which is a separate, uncontrolled confound.
findings.evidence: flores_replicate.py:168 char_cap=30000 then bc.romanize_cached at 224 / cap at 229; cache corpus/romanized/flores_bible_v2000_c30000 romanized lengths min=29941 max=65542 (Korean), ratio 2.19x.

findings.title: The 'content-controlled parallel Bible' backbone is New-Testament-only for everyone, so 'full Bible' coverage is illusory and the Hebrew data point is a modern NT translation, not Biblical Hebrew
findings.what_might_be_wrong: load(align=True) intersects verse IDs across all languages; because 9 of the 57 SELECTION files are NT-only (Ukranian-NT, Latvian-NT, Amharic, Syriac-NT, Swahili-NT, Zulu-NT, Basque-NT, plus Dutch/Shona with empty Full), the common backbone collapses to the New Testament. I verified the 6741 common verses are entirely NT books (MAT, MAR, LUK, JOH, ACT, ROM, 1CO, REV, ...; no Genesis/OT). So even the 48 'full' Bibles only ever contribute NT text, and the per-language character budget is set by NT content.
findings.why: Two consequences: (1) any framing that distinguishes full-Bible vs NT-only coverage, or that claims a large content budget, is misleading -- the experiment uses NT for everyone. (2) The 'Hebrew' language here is a MODERN Hebrew translation OF THE GREEK NEW TESTAMENT (the original Hebrew Bible has no NT), so it is neither Biblical Hebrew nor a neutral modern sample; it is translationese from Greek. This matters for any register/archaism caveat and for the Semitic-recovery interpretation.
findings.evidence: biblecorpus.py:73-76 intersection; SELECTION has 9 non-full files (verified via metadata Full column); diagnostic: common backbone book codes are all NT (1CO,JOH,MAT,...,REV), no OT books.

findings.title: Cross-script Bible pairs saturate at JS~1.0 (923/1596 pairs) and 9/57 languages get an arbitrary tie-broken nearest neighbour
findings.what_might_be_wrong: On the raw 57-language Bible, languages in different scripts share essentially only the space trigram, so JS saturates near 1.0: 923 of 1596 pairs (58%) fall in (0.999, 1.0001]. nn_purity (langtree.py:400) uses min(...) which returns the FIRST candidate on ties. I measured 9/57 languages whose nearest neighbour is an exact numerical tie, so their NN -- and their purity contribution -- depends on label/dict ordering, not on a real distinction.
findings.why: The prior audit flagged this for the small Tier-2 set and assumed the corpus swap mooted it; it persists at full Bible scale. Saturation means the raw cross-script topology and ~9 NN assignments are arbitrary tie-breaks rather than signal, so the raw-arm purity and dendrogram cross-script structure are partly artifacts of ordering and should not be presented as findings (this is also why the romanized arm looks dramatically better -- it removes a degeneracy, not only adds signal).
findings.evidence: Diagnostic on Bible js_matrix(n=3): 923/1596 pairs in (0.999,1.0001]; 9/57 languages have an exact-tie minimum-distance neighbour; nn_purity uses min(key=...) (first-wins) at langtree.py:400.

findings.title: rf_corrected normRF has an undisclosed achievable floor ~0.40 (not 0); the docstring's 'refinement not over-penalised / 0 = perfect' claim is false
findings.what_might_be_wrong: rf_corrected (langtree.py:436-450) normalizes by denom = inferred_bipartitions + gold_bipartitions and uses dendropy symmetric_difference. The gold tree is heavily multifurcating (23 non-trivial bipartitions for 57 taxa) while every inferred UPGMA/NJ tree is strictly binary (54 bipartitions). A binary tree therefore MUST add 54-23=31 splits that gold lacks, and dendropy counts every one of them in the symmetric difference. So the minimum achievable normRF is 31/(54+23)=31/77=0.403 — normRF can never reach 0, and the metric's real dynamic range is only [0.40, 1.0], not [0,1]. The docstring (line 439-440) explicitly claims 'a binary tree merely refining gold's polytomies is not over-penalised' and '0 = no conflicting splits', both of which are wrong.
findings.why: The headline tree-quality numbers (UPGMA 0.792, NJ 0.714) are presented as fractions where 0=perfect, implying the trees are ~70-80% wrong. In reality they sit only ~0.31-0.39 above the unavoidable floor of 0.40, on a scale whose ceiling (random null) is ~0.97. Reading 0.71/0.79 against 0 systematically understates tree quality, and the docstring guarantees a reader/grader will misinterpret '0=perfect'. The prior audit RECOMMENDED exactly this denominator as THE fix for 'a perfect tree can't score 0', so the implemented fix does not deliver what was promised — the floor moved but did not vanish.
findings.evidence: Probe 19: gold_bp=23, binary_bp=54 for n=57 -> floor = 31/77 = 0.403. Probe 19b: gold=((a,b),c,d,e) vs zero-conflict refinement ((a,b),(c,(d,e))) gives rf_corrected normRF=0.333 (rf=1>0) despite no conflicting split. langtree.py:439-440 docstring text.
findings.lens: code-correctness (adversarial re-read of langtree.py internals + live probes on copies in /private/tmp)

findings.title: nn_purity nearest-neighbour is decided by arbitrary index/dict order for the 9 unique-script languages tied at JS=1.0, distorting the RAW family-purity headline
findings.what_might_be_wrong: nn_purity (langtree.py:395-402) picks the NN with j=min(range, key=lambda k: D[i,k]). On the RAW 57-language arm, every unique-script language (Greek, Kannada, Malayalam, Telugu, Hebrew, Syriac, Korean, Thai, Burmese) has JS=1.000 to ALL 56 others (disjoint trigram vocabularies). min() then returns whichever language is FIRST in the names list — which is English. So 9/57 languages have a meaningless NN dictated purely by list order, and Greek is scored a 'family hit' only because English happens to be Indo-European and first in the list.
findings.why: The RAW family-NN purity headline (claimed 0.65, reproduced as 0.667) is the baseline the whole 'romanization helps' story is measured against. 9 of its 57 data points are arbitrary tie-breaks, not findings. Reordering the language list (first- vs last-index tie-break) changes which ties become hits and swings purity by 0.018 in my probe; a different ordering that put a same-family language first for Hebrew/Syriac (Afro-Asiatic), the Dravidian trio, or Burmese could inflate it further (those families have a same-family member among the tied set). The number is therefore order-dependent and partly an artifact of disjoint-vocab degeneracy rather than measured similarity.
findings.evidence: Probe 14: 9/57 NN are ties, all at JS=1.000; first-index purity 0.667 vs last-index 0.649. Probe (tie list): all 9 unique-script langs report first-winner=English (IE); Greek scored hit by luck, others miss; Hebrew/Syriac/Kannada/Malayalam/Telugu/Burmese have a same-family member among the tied 56 so a different order could flip them to hits. Probe 1b toy: purity flips 0.333->0.000 purely from reordering tied items.
findings.lens: code-correctness (adversarial re-read of langtree.py internals + live probes on copies in /private/tmp)

findings.title: Family-NN purity uses the coarse 'Family' label (Indo-European = 54% of the panel), inflating the headline and hiding a 0.31 chance floor
findings.what_might_be_wrong: All purity calls (bible_poc.py, bible_romanize.py, build_notebook.py via fam_of = {r[0]: r[1]}) group by the metadata 'Family' column. In the 57-language panel Indo-European is 31/57 = 54% of all languages, so a Hindi->Russian, Greek->English, or Farsi->Polish nearest neighbour all count as 'family hits' across genealogically very deep IE branches. The romanized headline purity of 0.772 drops to 0.667 when scored at the genus/branch level (Germanic/Romance/Slavic/Indo-Iranian...).
findings.why: The 0.77 (and raw 0.65) purity headlines are presented as evidence the method recovers language families, but with a 54%-IE panel the random-NN chance floor is 0.31, not ~0, and much of the 'success' is just 'this IE language's nearest neighbour is some other IE language'. The absolute number is favorable framing: the genuine branch-level recovery is 0.667, and the margin over the alphabet baseline (which is also scored against the same coarse bucket, so the margin is apples-to-apples) is the only defensible claim. The prior audit flagged a coarse-IE bucket only for the retired 9-language Tier-2 panel; here it recurs, unfixed, on the current 57-language crux with the chance floor (0.31) never reported.
findings.evidence: Probe 16: family-level NN purity 0.772 vs genus-level 0.667 on the romanized arm; chance floor family-level 0.308 vs genus-level 0.067; IE = 31/57 = 0.54. Probe 3: family composition (IE 31, Austronesian 5, Afro-Asiatic 4, ...).
findings.lens: code-correctness (adversarial re-read of langtree.py internals + live probes on copies in /private/tmp)

findings.title: ncd_matrix_fixed subtracts a single panel-averaged self-compression floor from every pairwise distance, which can clamp genuine close-relative signal to 0
findings.what_might_be_wrong: ncd_matrix_fixed (langtree.py:299-319) computes a per-language self-NCD floor (gz(b+SEP+b)-C)/C, AVERAGES it across all languages into one scalar `floor`, then applies D[i,j] = max(0.0, d - floor) to every pair. Subtracting one global constant from all distances and clamping at 0 collapses any genuine pairwise distance below the floor to exactly 0, destroying resolution among close relatives, and the uniform shift is not a metric-preserving transform (it can invert the ordering of very close pairs relative to the cap).
findings.why: The gzip-NCD arm is the 'independent estimator' cross-check (claimed normRF 0.59 in the notebook, r=0.78 vs ASJP). If close-relative NCDs are clamped to 0 by the floor subtraction, the NCD dendrogram's fine structure (which pairs are closest) is degraded, weakening exactly the agreement the cross-check is supposed to demonstrate. The floor is also computed from the romanized texts whose self-similarity differs from cross-pair similarity, so the subtracted constant is not the right correction for any individual pair.
findings.evidence: langtree.py:310-318: floor = mean of per-language floors; D[i,j] = max(0.0, d - floor). Probe 15 confirms the clamp path runs and a near-duplicate pair collapses toward 0 (0.0131); the max(0.0, d-floor) means any pair with raw d<floor becomes exactly 0.
findings.lens: code-correctness (adversarial re-read of langtree.py internals + live probes on copies in /private/tmp)

findings.title: Confirmed NOT bugs (so they are not re-chased): Turkish casefold fix, JS implementation, gold-tree construction, NJ validity, random-tree uniformity, bootstrap resampling
findings.what_might_be_wrong: Several items that could plausibly be wrong were probed and are correct, so reviewer effort should not be spent re-opening them. (a) clean() now NFC-normalizes then deletes U+0307, so the prior-audit Turkish casefold bug is genuinely fixed and the blanket U+0307 removal has zero collateral damage on this corpus. (b) js_div_counts (sparse, base-2, sqrt) matches scipy jensenshannon to 1e-16 and correctly returns 1.0 for disjoint vocab, 0 for identical, 1.0 for empty. (c) gold_newick_from_rows nests Family->Genus->Subgenus correctly and collapses single-child levels properly. (d) nj_newick produces valid parseable Newick with 54 bipartitions. (e) _random_binary_newick is ~uniform over topologies for small n (ratio 1.03 at 5 leaves). (f) bootstrap_ci/branch_support multinomial resample drops zero-count keys, which is harmless for js_div_counts.
findings.why: The prior audit listed the Turkish casefold and several JS/NCD concerns; verifying they are now correct prevents the parent agent from spending effort on already-resolved or non-existent issues, and isolates the genuine remaining problems (the three above) for action.
findings.evidence: Probe 12: clean('İstanbul')=='istanbul', clean('İSTANBUL İNSAN')=='istanbul insan'. Probe 13: U+0307 appears only in Turkish (264 occ) post-casefold. Probe 9: max|dense-sparse JS| = 1.11e-16. Probe 10/11: disjoint=1.0, identical=0, empty=1.0. Probe 20: gold nesting correct. Probe 22: NJ 54 bipartitions, parses. Probe 2b: 15 topologies, freq ratio 1.032. Probe 21: zero-drop identical JS.
findings.lens: code-correctness (adversarial re-read of langtree.py internals + live probes on copies in /private/tmp)

research.theme: Distance-estimation: plug-in bias of character-n-gram Jensen-Shannon / KL divergence from finite text, small-sample correction, smoothing for sparse n-grams, choosing n, length-normalization for cross-language comparability, and whether ~2k parallel sentences suffice. Tied to the adversarial review's "estimation-bias" critical findings (#1, #14, the K/N artifact, the n-order overclaim) and the FLORES data-sufficiency findings.

root_causes.issue: Plug-in (MLE) Jensen-Shannon distance from finite counts is systematically biased UPWARD, and the bias scales with the number of occupied trigram cells K and inversely with text length N — exactly the differential bias the review measured (r=0.40 with max(K) and r=0.37 with |K_i-K_j|).
root_causes.explanation: This is a direct corollary of the classic entropy/mutual-information bias result. The plug-in entropy estimator H_hat is NEGATIVELY biased by approximately -(K-1)/(2N) to first order (the Miller-Madow term; Roulston 1999; Panzeri & Treves 1996), where K = number of cells with non-zero probability and N = sample size. JS divergence is JS = H(mean) - mean(H), a difference of entropies, so the negative entropy bias does NOT cancel: the mixture entropy H((P+Q)/2) sits over a larger occupied support than either component, so its downward bias is smaller in magnitude than the average of the two component downward biases, leaving JS biased UPWARD by O(K/N). The JSD-estimation thesis (Charlotte 2023) confirms the plug-in JSD bias is O(1/N) and reducible to O(1/N^2) by jackknife. Because two empirical distributions over a sparse high-dimensional trigram space share few rare trigrams by chance alone, even two SAMPLES FROM THE SAME LANGUAGE register a non-zero JS; the apparent 'divergence' is partly just non-overlap of rare-trigram tails. K and N co-vary with script and morphology (CJK/agglutinative -> huge K; short or large-inventory text -> bad K/N), and script/morphology co-vary with family, so the bias loads onto the very axis being tested. A measurable fraction of the recovered 'genealogy' is a vocab-size/length artifact, not inheritance.

root_causes.issue: No smoothing of the sparse trigram distributions — raw MLE relative frequencies with hard zeros for unseen trigrams.
root_causes.explanation: langtree.py's js_div_counts / ngram_dist use raw c.get(g,0)/tot, so every trigram unseen in a text gets probability exactly 0. In a trigram space of thousands of types estimated from a few thousand sentences, the vast majority of cells are zero, and which cells are zero is a sampling accident. JS handles zeros gracefully (unlike KL) but it still treats every unobserved trigram as evidence of difference, which is precisely what inflates cross-language and cross-script distances and produces the JS=1.0 saturation for disjoint scripts (review's 'Amharic magnet'). The standard NLP fix — smoothing (Laplace/Lidstone, Witten-Bell, Good-Turing, and especially interpolated/modified Kneser-Ney) — exists specifically because unsmoothed higher-order n-gram MLE is unreliable on held-out data; Kneser-Ney reduces perplexity 30-40% vs add-k on natural text. Gamallo et al. 2017, the canonical character-n-gram language-distance paper, explicitly smooth (interpolation) for this reason. Unsmoothed trigram-JS is below the field's minimum bar.

root_causes.issue: The distance is a raw JS over relative frequencies with no length normalization or per-language standardization, so distances are not on a comparable scale across languages of different N and K.
root_causes.explanation: Because the plug-in JS bias is a function of (K_i, K_j, N_i, N_j), a language with short text or a large alphabet has an inflated distance to EVERY other language, not just its true relatives. There is no mechanism in the pipeline to remove this per-language offset. The field standard for n-gram language distance (Gamallo, Pichel, Alegria 2017, Physica A 484:162-172) is NOT raw divergence but PERPLEXITY of one language's smoothed character model evaluated on the other's text, symmetrized — perplexity is the exponential of a per-symbol (length-normalized) cross-entropy, which automatically normalizes for test-text length and is far more robust to the finite-sample issues than a raw divergence over relative frequencies. Bentz et al. 2017 go further and hold the corpus identical (Parallel Bible Corpus, same register) AND fix the token budget at the empirically determined convergence point so that N is constant across languages.

root_causes.issue: n=3 is asserted ('stable across n') rather than chosen by an estimator-quality criterion; higher n strictly worsens the K/N sparsity/bias, and unigrams may already carry most of the signal.
root_causes.explanation: For fixed N, raising n explodes K (possible trigrams >> possible unigrams), so the (K-1)/(2N) bias and the fraction of zero cells both grow with n — higher order buys linguistic dependency information at the cost of catastrophically worse estimation. The bias-variance tradeoff for n-gram order is real and the standard way to pick n is held-out perplexity / cross-validation, NOT an RF-argmin or an eyeballed 'stability' sweep. If unigram-JS (frequencies only, K small, low bias) matches trigram-JS on the tree metric, the higher-order information-theoretic content is demonstrably NOT what is driving the result, which undercuts the project's central 'information theory earns its keep' claim. The review is correct that 'stable across n' can mean 'the trigram machinery adds little over unigrams while incurring the worst bias.'

root_causes.issue: ~2,009 FLORES sentences are too few for character-trigram distributions over large-inventory scripts; the review's K/N=0.77 (Chinese), 0.37 (Korean) put those estimates in the near-pure-noise regime.
root_causes.explanation: Entropy/divergence estimation is reliable only when sampling is dense relative to the alphabet. The accepted danger zones: classical estimators need N >> K; Bayesian estimators (NSB, Hausser-Strimmer shrinkage) extend usability only down to roughly N ~ sqrt(K) by inverting the birthday-paradox / coincidence count, and below that even they degrade. A K/N near or above ~0.3-0.8 means most trigrams are singletons or unseen, so the empirical distribution is dominated by which rare trigrams happened to appear — JS between two such near-singleton distributions measures sampling accidents, not language. This is distinct from the prior audit's UDHR-truncation point and is quantitatively confirmed by the review. Bentz et al. 2017 specifically determined an empirical token-convergence threshold (on the order of tens of thousands of tokens for WORD entropy; character distributions stabilize faster but CJK/Korean trigram inventories are large) before trusting estimates — the project never establishes such a threshold.

solutions.approach: Replace plug-in JS with a bias-corrected divergence: estimate each language's character distribution with a low-bias entropy/divergence estimator (NSB Bayesian, or Miller-Madow / Grassberger / Chao-Shen coverage-adjusted), then form JS from the corrected component and mixture entropies. NSB is the cross-linguistics field choice (Bentz et al. 2017 use it for exactly this cross-language comparability problem; Arora/Meister/Cotterell 2022 recommend Bayesian/coverage-adjusted estimators over MLE for sparse linguistic distributions).
solutions.fixes: Root causes #1 (upward plug-in JS bias scaling with K and N) and #5 (undersampled FLORES estimates). Directly addresses review findings #1, #14 and the K-correlation artifact.
solutions.tradeoffs: NSB is computationally heavier (numerical integration) and needs the alphabet-size hyperparameter; on JS you must apply it to all three entropy terms. Miller-Madow is a trivial closed-form add-(K-1)/2N correction (cheapest) but only first-order and assumes the support is known. Coverage-adjusted (Chao-Shen) is consistent and handles unseen mass but has higher variance. None fully removes bias when K/N is extreme — pair with a data-budget fix.
solutions.reference: Arora, Meister, Cotterell, 'Estimating the Entropy of Linguistic Distributions', ACL 2022, https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-short.20/ (code https://github.com/rycolab/entropy-estimation); Nemenman, Shafee, Bialek (NSB) entropy estimator; Roulston 1999, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167278998002693 ; Vu, Yu, Kass, coverage-adjusted, https://www.stat.berkeley.edu/users/binyu/ps/entropy.sub.pdf

solutions.approach: Switch the distance itself to the field-standard perplexity / cross-entropy measure: build a smoothed character-n-gram model per language and compute symmetric per-symbol cross-entropy (or perplexity) of each model on the other's text. This is length-normalized by construction and is the established method for n-gram language distance.
solutions.fixes: Root causes #1 (bias), #3 (no length normalization / cross-language comparability), and partially #2 (forces you to smooth). Addresses the review's 'distances not comparable across languages' and the JS-saturation degeneracy.
solutions.tradeoffs: Perplexity-based distance is not a true metric without symmetrization (Gamallo symmetrizes the two directional perplexities); it requires a held-out split (train model on part, evaluate on held-out part) which costs data; and it still needs a smoothing choice. But it is exactly what the canonical paper does and sidesteps the raw-frequency-JS pathologies.
solutions.reference: Gamallo, Pichel, Alegria, 'From language identification to language distance', Physica A 484 (2017) 162-172, https://gramatica.usc.es/~gamallo/artigos-web/PHYSICA2017.pdf ; reference implementation uses character 7-grams + interpolation smoothing, https://github.com/gamallo/Perplexity

solutions.approach: Smooth the trigram distributions before computing any divergence: at minimum interpolated/modified Kneser-Ney (best for character n-grams), or as a cheap stopgap Lidstone/add-lambda or Witten-Bell. Keep JS but feed it smoothed distributions.
solutions.fixes: Root cause #2 (hard zeros) and mitigates #1 (smoothing shrinks the rare-trigram noise that drives upward bias) and the JS=1.0 cross-script saturation degeneracy (review's tie-break / 'Amharic magnet' findings).
solutions.tradeoffs: Add-one/Lidstone is known to perform poorly on natural text (over-smooths, distorts the head); Kneser-Ney is materially better but more code. Smoothing reduces but does not eliminate the K/N bias, and the smoothing constant is another hyperparameter to set by held-out perplexity. Cheapest single improvement that lifts the method to the field's minimum standard.
solutions.reference: Chen & Goodman, 'An Empirical Study of Smoothing Techniques for Language Modeling' (modified Kneser-Ney best); summary https://aclanthology.org/P96-1041/ ; character-level KN for language ID, survey https://arxiv.org/pdf/1804.08186

solutions.approach: Fix the data budget and report the K/N diagnostic: hold N (characters or tokens) constant across all languages by truncating to a common budget set at an empirically determined convergence point, and exclude or flag any language whose trigram K/N exceeds a sufficiency threshold (e.g. report the singleton fraction f1/N and the coincidence count). Run a held-out convergence curve (distance vs N) to find where distances stabilize.
solutions.fixes: Root cause #5 (FLORES too small for CJK/Korean) and #3 (unequal N across languages, including the romanized-arm budget-set-before-expansion bug the review flagged). Establishes the missing data-sufficiency justification.
solutions.tradeoffs: Truncating to the smallest language's budget wastes data and may push everyone into the undersampled zone; better to set the budget at the convergence knee and drop languages that cannot reach it. Reporting K/N and convergence curves is cheap and is what reviewers will expect, but it may force you to admit the FLORES arm cannot support CJK/Korean trigram distances — that honesty is itself a fix.
solutions.reference: Bentz, Alikaniotis, Cysouw, Ferrer-i-Cancho, 'The Entropy of Words', Entropy 19(6):275 (2017), https://doi.org/10.3390/e19060275 (empirical token-convergence threshold + NSB + identical-register Parallel Bible Corpus)

solutions.approach: Justify (or revise) n by an estimator-quality criterion: select n by held-out per-symbol cross-entropy / perplexity (cross-validated), and report the unigram-only distance alongside trigram as an ablation. If unigram ~ trigram on the tree metric, reframe the contribution honestly (frequency signal, not higher-order dependency).
solutions.fixes: Root cause #4 (unjustified n=3, 'stable across n' overclaim). Directly addresses the review's interpretation-overclaim finding on n-gram order.
solutions.tradeoffs: Held-out perplexity may select a LOWER n than 3 once estimation quality is weighed, weakening the 'information-theoretic structure' narrative; but it replaces an RF-argmin / eyeball justification with a principled one. Minimal compute.
solutions.reference: Standard LM practice: pick order by held-out perplexity / cross-validation under the bias-variance tradeoff; Chen & Goodman (above); n-gram sparsity grows exponentially with n (Lidstone needed), https://medium.com/mti-technology/n-gram-language-models-70af02e742ad

solutions.approach: Quantify the residual bias with a proper resampling scheme: use a JACKKNIFE bias correction on the JS estimate (reduces bias O(1/N)->O(1/N^2)) and replace the invalid multinomial token bootstrap with a BLOCK / sentence-level bootstrap that resamples whole aligned sentences (respecting trigram overlap dependence) to get honest, mis-centering-aware CIs.
solutions.fixes: The review's 'significance' findings about the invalid bootstrap (falsely tight, mis-centered CIs) plus residual #1 bias. Makes the reported purity/CI numbers defensible.
solutions.tradeoffs: Jackknife and block-bootstrap are more expensive (refit per fold/replicate) and the block size is a choice; jackknife corrects bias but can inflate variance. Crucially, resampling alone cannot reveal the bias unless the underlying estimator is also bias-corrected — combine with solution 1. Block bootstrap fixes the independence violation that shrank the old CIs.
solutions.reference: JSD plug-in bias O(1/N), jackknife to O(1/N^2): 'Jensen-Shannon Divergence: Estimation and Hypothesis Testing' thesis, https://ninercommons.charlotte.edu/record/1093 ; for dependence, sentence/block bootstrap over aligned units rather than i.i.d. trigram multinomial.

research.field_standard: The established practice for measuring language distance from raw text with character n-grams is NOT raw plug-in Jensen-Shannon over unsmoothed relative frequencies. The canonical method (Gamallo, Pichel, Alegria 2017, Physica A) builds a SMOOTHED character n-gram model per language (they use 7-grams with interpolation) and measures distance as the symmetrized per-symbol cross-entropy / PERPLEXITY of one language's model on the other's text — a length-normalized quantity that is comparable across languages and far more robust to finite-sample sparsity than a raw divergence. For the entropy/divergence quantities themselves, the cross-linguistics standard (Bentz et al. 2017, Entropy) is to (a) use a low-bias estimator — the NSB Bayesian estimator, not plug-in MLE — because plug-in is biased by approximately -(K-1)/2N (Miller-Madow / Roulston), (b) hold register and content constant via a parallel corpus, and (c) fix the token budget at an empirically determined convergence point so N is identical across languages. Arora, Meister & Cotterell (ACL 2022) is the explicit warning that over-reliance on poor (plug-in) estimators OVER-ESTIMATES effect sizes in information-theoretic linguistics and that estimator choice should depend on distribution type and data availability; for sparse distributions they favor Bayesian/coverage-adjusted estimators. Unseen n-grams must be SMOOTHED (interpolated/modified Kneser-Ney is the best-performing standard; Chen & Goodman). n is chosen by held-out perplexity / cross-validation, not by eyeballed stability or an RF-argmin. Sufficiency: estimates are trustworthy only when sampling is dense relative to the alphabet (classical estimators need N >> K; even NSB-class estimators only reach down to N ~ sqrt(K)); a trigram K/N near or above ~0.3-0.8, as the review found for FLORES Chinese/Korean, is the undersampled noise regime and should be flagged or excluded. In short: smooth, length-normalize (perplexity/cross-entropy), bias-correct (NSB/Miller-Madow/Chao-Shen), equalize N, justify n by held-out perplexity, and report K/N sufficiency — the project's current raw-MLE-trigram-JS pipeline does none of these and is below the field bar on every count.

citations.source: Gamallo, Pichel, Alegria (2017), 'From language identification to language distance', Physica A 484:162-172
citations.key_point: Canonical character-n-gram language-distance method: SMOOTHED 7-gram character models + symmetrized PERPLEXITY (length-normalized cross-entropy), not raw plug-in JS. Validated on 44 European languages and against phylogenetic networks.

citations.source: Gamallo Perplexity reference implementation (GitHub README)
citations.key_point: Confirms the method: 'based on 7-grams models of characters and interpolation for smoothing'; test corpus shorter than train (e.g. 1Mb train / 25K test) — i.e. held-out, length-normalized.

citations.source: Bentz, Alikaniotis, Cysouw, Ferrer-i-Cancho (2017), 'The Entropy of Words', Entropy 19(6):275
citations.key_point: Cross-linguistic standard: uses the NSB estimator (faster convergence, less bias than MLE), determines an empirical token-convergence threshold from a 21-language parallel set before trusting estimates, and uses the Parallel Bible Corpus to hold register constant.

citations.source: Arora, Meister, Cotterell (2022), 'Estimating the Entropy of Linguistic Distributions', ACL 2022
citations.key_point: Over-reliance on POOR entropy estimators (plug-in MLE) OVER-ESTIMATES effect sizes in information-theoretic linguistics; gives concrete estimator recommendations by distribution type and data availability (favoring Bayesian/coverage-adjusted for sparse data). Code at rycolab/entropy-estimation.

citations.source: Roulston (1999), 'Estimating the errors on measured entropy and mutual information', Physica D
citations.key_point: Plug-in entropy is biased by ~-(K-1)/2N (K = occupied cells, N = samples); the bias propagates as an UPWARD bias in divergence/MI and is the analytic basis for the review's K- and N-dependent JS artifact.

citations.source: 'Jensen-Shannon Divergence: Estimation and Hypothesis Testing' (thesis, UNC Charlotte, 2023)
citations.key_point: Plug-in JSD bias is O(1/N); jackknife reduces it to O(1/N^2). Confirms plug-in JS over-estimates and is fixable by resampling-based bias correction — directly relevant to the project's invalid token bootstrap.

citations.source: Vu, Yu, Kass, 'Coverage-adjusted entropy estimation' (Chao-Shen / Good-Turing)
citations.key_point: MLE entropy is negatively biased; the coverage-adjusted estimator (Horvitz-Thompson + Good-Turing coverage 1 - f1/N) is consistent and first-order optimal — a low-effort alternative to NSB for unseen-mass correction.

citations.source: 'Entropy estimators for Markovian sequences: A comparative analysis' (2024), Entropy / arXiv 2310.07547
citations.key_point: For sequential/symbol data the MLE bias is -(N0-1)/2N plus a Markov correlation term; in the undersampled regime NSB and BHM (and coverage-adjusted CC) dominate on bias and MSE — guidance for choosing the corrected estimator for TEXT trigrams.

citations.source: Chen & Goodman (1996/1999), 'An Empirical Study of Smoothing Techniques for Language Modeling'
citations.key_point: Interpolated/modified Kneser-Ney is the best-performing smoothing; add-one/Lidstone is substantially worse on natural text. Unsmoothed higher-order n-gram MLE (the project's choice) is below the smoothing standard; KN cuts perplexity 30-40% vs add-k.

citations.source: Survey: 'Automatic Language Identification in Texts' (arXiv 1804.08186)
citations.key_point: Character n-gram language models for language ID use modified Kneser-Ney; character 3-5-grams are the workhorse but require smoothing — supports both the smoothing fix and the n-order discussion.

research.theme: tree-and-significance: estimation bias, tree-distance scaling, and significance/null/baseline validity in an information-theoretic language-tree pipeline
research.field_standard: The defensible workflow that comparative-linguistic phylogenetics actually uses, mapped to this project: (1) Distances. Either accept that character-n-gram JS is a surface/orthographic similarity (not additive, not bias-controlled) and validate it as such, or correct it: bias-aware divergence (jackknifed/coverage-adjusted JS, or build distances from bias-corrected block entropies you already have via block_entropy_grassberger), plus a transcription arm (uroman/epitran) to remove the script confound that drives cross-script JS saturation. (2) Trees. Distance methods (UPGMA assumes a molecular clock/ultrametric; NJ assumes additivity, Saitou-Nei 1987, Atteson 1997 consistency requires near-additivity that JS violates) are acceptable for an exploratory tree, but the gold standard in serious language phylogenetics is character/cognate-based Bayesian inference (BEAST2, MrBayes; Gray-Atkinson 2003, Bouckaert 2012, Heggarty 2023) with a posterior distribution rather than one point tree. For a course project, keep NJ but derive ALL reliability numbers (cophenetic, bootstrap support, CIs) from the SAME tree you headline, and bootstrap by resampling the DATA UNITS (characters/sites with replacement, Felsenstein 1985), never the fitted distribution. (3) Scoring vs a Glottolog gold tree. The field standard for scoring an inferred tree against a polytomous reference is the Generalized Quartet Distance (Pompei-Loreto-Tria 2011, also used by Jaeger 2018), which does not penalize resolving the reference's polytomies and has a clean [0,1] scale with random-tree expectation ~1/3; Robinson-Foulds is acceptable only if you report the achievable floor (RF of a perfect refinement of the gold polytomies) and the saturated random-null (~1.0 on polytomous gold), and present the triple (observed, floor, null). Build the gold topology programmatically from Glottolog (pyglottolog / Glottolog Newick), not from URIEL/lang2vec, whose family vectors are flattened one-hot and lose hierarchy. (4) Significance. Use chance-corrected metrics: Adjusted Rand Index or B-cubed instead of raw nearest-neighbour purity (purity has a high non-zero chance floor under class imbalance and is not chance-corrected); score at genus level so the 31/57 Indo-European bucket does not dominate; report every headline scalar against its own permutation/random baseline; use a Mantel permutation test (not a naive Pearson p) for matrix-vs-matrix correlation, ideally a partial/within-block Mantel so coarse script/major-family agreement cannot masquerade as fine-structure agreement.

solutions.approach: Switch the primary tree-vs-gold metric from normalized RF to Generalized Quartet Distance (GQD), which ignores unresolved (star) quartets in the polytomous gold so resolving polytomies is not penalized; report GQD in [0,1] with its ~1/3 random-tree expectation. Keep RF only as a secondary number reported as a TRIPLE (observed, achievable floor from a perfect refinement of the resolved gold, random-null p50).
solutions.fixes: RF floor ~0.40 unreachable-by-construction; random-null mis-quoted as 0.97; all method/corpus comparisons on the wrong yardstick; the docstring '0=perfect' that misleads a grader.
solutions.tradeoffs: GQD needs a quartet-counting implementation (tqDist, or dendropy/ete3 quartet routines) and an explanation in the writeup; it changes every headline number. But it is the actual field standard for scoring language trees against polytomous classifications, so it strengthens the defense rather than weakens it. Computing the RF floor empirically (RF of a random binary refinement of the gold) is cheap and can be done immediately even if GQD is deferred.
solutions.reference: Pompei, Loreto & Tria 2011, 'On the Accuracy of Language Trees', PLoS ONE 6(6):e20109 (arXiv 1103.4012); also used in Jaeger 2018, Sci. Data 5:180189. tqDist quartet distance tool.

solutions.approach: Replace the distribution-resampling bootstrap with a proper site/character bootstrap: resample the aligned character (or verse) units with replacement, re-clean, re-count n-grams, re-infer the tree, and count edge recurrence (Felsenstein bootstrap). Apply the SAME procedure to put CIs on distances, GQD/RF, and purity, and report branch support on the HEADLINE (NJ) tree.
solutions.fixes: Falsely tight, mis-centered CIs from resampling the plug-in distribution; bootstrap supports that ignore n-gram dependence; reliability numbers computed on UPGMA while NJ is the headline.
solutions.tradeoffs: Requires the corpus to be aligned into resamplable units (FLORES/verse-aligned Bible already are), and is slower than the multinomial draw. It still cannot remove the estimation BIAS (only the variance) so it must be paired with bias correction or with an honest 'this measures surface similarity' framing; CIs will be wider and some headline gaps may stop being significant, which is the correct outcome.
solutions.reference: Felsenstein 1985, 'Confidence limits on phylogenies: an approach using the bootstrap', Evolution 39:783-791; modern refinement Lemoine et al. 2018, Nature 556:452 (transfer bootstrap expectation).

solutions.approach: Report chance-corrected agreement metrics: replace raw nearest-neighbour purity with Adjusted Rand Index or B-cubed against family labels, score at the GENUS level so the 31/57 Indo-European bucket cannot dominate, and always print the random/permutation baseline next to each number. Fix the gold labels (collapse 'Altaic'/'Altaic(?)', the Icelandic script error) and acknowledge the singleton-family ceiling.
solutions.fixes: Purity inflated by 54%-IE class imbalance; ~0.31 chance floor never reported; coarse Family label crediting deep splits as hits; typo-split and singleton-family gold inconsistencies that distort both purity and RF.
solutions.tradeoffs: ARI/B-cubed are less intuitive to a lay reader than 'purity' and the corrected numbers will be lower and less flattering, but they are the standard chance-corrected external-validation metrics and remove the single most exploitable objection.
